text: and forced her eyes to remain on his. “I probably wasted a lot already .” “As much water as there is in Cheyenne, it’s still a limited resource .” “Why do you even need all this?” She spread her arms wide, indicating everything . “I don’t .” “But you have it. Clean, running water. Beds. Toilets . This place must have ten separate rooms !” “I have a place where I can clean my weapons and tend to my gear when I’m not in the Dust. Everything else is superfluous .” “What does super…what does that mean ?” “It means it’s unnecessary .” “Well, why didn’t you just say that? Either way, it’s all running . In good shape. And it’s necessary for us .” Lara swept her gaze around. There were more luxuries in this one room than she’d known throughout her entire life . “I bring in scrap and sell it. Got nothing to do with keeping this place running, except for providing some raw materials. There are other bots that handle the upkeep. They’re programmed to repair buildings, clean houses, cut grass, and keep appliances running. The Creators shaped them for those tasks, and they’ll perform them until they eventually break down .” “Is going into the Dust your purpose?” Her gaze dipped, moving over the line of his jaw to his thick neck . “Collecting trinkets yours ?” She met his eyes. “My purpose is to survive. I don’t go out of my way, those are just things that crossed my path .” “All paths in this word lead to dust. One way, or another .” “You’re evading!” The bots Lara had dealt with were always so blunt and direct. Why was this one so different ? “What does it matter? I’m surviving, same as you .” “Because I’m curious. You said there are bots made for only one purpose, and they know nothing else. But if you knew your purpose, you’d be doing it right now .” “How do you know I’m not fulfilling my programming right now ?” Lara narrowed her eyes. “And what programming would that be ?” “Evading your line of questioning .” She growled in frustration. “That is such a hu—” Something on his abdomen caught her attention, and she snapped her mouth shut. Everything about him screamed human male — his broad chest with its light brown nipples, his toned shoulders and arms, the sculpted ridges of his abdomen. Like the synth dancers in Kitty’s, he lacked a belly button, but there were three holes in the skin of his belly. Dull metal gleamed through them. “Are those…bullet holes ?” Ronin looked down. “I’m much better at dodging questions than I am other things, obviously .” “That happen in the Dust?” Lara stepped around the central counter and approached him, attention focused on the wounds — if they could even be called that . “ Yes .” She leaned down to examine the holes. The metal visible in each was intact but shiny, as though it had been freshly polished. Her brows fell; could bots feel pain? Had Ronin felt pain? Without thinking, she reached forward to touch the damaged skin . No touching . Lara stopped her hand before making contact and straightened. She sure as hell wasn’t going to give him an excuse to break that part of their deal. “What’s out there ?” The muscles — she knew he didn’t actually have any, but she didn’t have another name for the parts that moved his face — of his jaw bunched before he spoke. “The name says it all. Not much of anything but dirt, settled over the top of the old world. If you’re willing to dig, there are things of value hidden all over .” “How far have you gone ?” “How high can you count ?” Lara glared at him . “It’s a valid question .” “I’m not stupid .” Ronin’s eyes narrowed. “Never said you were .” “Damn near did .” “What does a number tell you if — ” “I can count,” she snapped. “I can’t read, but I can fucking count .” “To what? Millions? Trillions ?” “Just answer the damned question!” Her face heated. So what if she couldn’t count that high? He was probably just making up numbers to make her feel stupid . “I’ve gone just about everywhere that isn’t blocked by water. One million, two thousand, seven hundred and seventy-four miles. Or thereabouts .” It was a bigger number than she’d ever heard, which only increased its enormity to Lara. Still, something stirred inside of her, a dangerous feeling. Hope. If he’d truly gone so far, he’d seen other places. “It’s not all dust, right ?” “Most of it is,” Ronin replied, his expression softening. “Some places are better off than others. Cheyenne seems to have been spared the worst of it .” So this was as good as it got? “Why just this place ?” * * * “I didn’t say just this one. There are other towns where things are in order. Just like some were turned into craters where the rubble is so fine you can sift it through your fingers. Entire cities turned to dust .” Would it be better if he was unable to track the miles? If he couldn’t recall, with undiluted clarity, all the dead places he’d traversed? Somewhere, deep within his tattered data banks, memories of those places from before the Black Out awaited discovery . Uncovering those memories wasn’t likely to bring comfort . “Oh.” Lara glanced away, shoulders sagging. “Do you like what you do? Scavenging out in the Dust ?” Ronin’s processors whirred, analyzing her question and searching out the best answer. There wasn’t supposed to be like or dislike for him — only programming and its fulfillment . “It’s preferable to repairing buildings or mowing grass,” he finally replied . “So…you like being out there ?” How could he explain it to her?
